one_liner: A 3-year-old boy of Japanese descent is brought in with 6 days of high fever, a rash, red eyes, and a 'strawberry' tongue unresponsive to antipyretics and antibiotics.

stage2.hpi_extension: He has had a **high fever (up to 40 C) for 6 days** that has not responded to acetaminophen, ibuprofen, or a recent course of amoxicillin prescribed for presumed otitis media. His mother reports **bilateral red eyes without discharge**, a **polymorphous truncal rash**, **cracked red lips and a strawberry tongue**, **swollen red hands and feet**, and a **single enlarged tender lymph node on the right side of the neck**. He is extremely irritable.
stage2.medications: Acetaminophen, ibuprofen, amoxicillin (started 3 days ago with no improvement)
stage2.family_history: No family history of autoimmune or vasculitic disease
stage2.social_history: Attends preschool; no sick contacts; family originally from Japan
stage2.physical_exam: General: irritable, flushed child. HEENT: **bilateral nonexudative bulbar conjunctival injection with limbal sparing**; **cracked, fissured, bright red lips**; **strawberry tongue**. Neck: **unilateral anterior cervical lymphadenopathy (~1.8 cm)**. Skin: **polymorphous erythematous truncal rash**; **edema and erythema of the palms and soles**; early periungual desquamation beginning. CV: tachycardic, no murmur; **hyperdynamic precordium**. Abdomen: mild hepatomegaly.

lab_results.result: **ESR 78 mm/hr (markedly elevated)**; **CRP 14 mg/dL**; platelets: 620,000/microL (**thrombocytosis**); WBC 18,500 with left shift; mild normocytic anemia; **sterile pyuria on UA**; ALT mildly elevated.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated acute-phase reactants, thrombocytosis (especially in the second week), and sterile pyuria support the clinical diagnosis of Kawasaki disease, a medium-vessel vasculitis.

lab_results.result: **2D echocardiogram: mild dilation of the right coronary artery (Z-score 2.3)**; no aneurysm yet; normal ventricular function.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Coronary artery involvement is the feared complication of Kawasaki disease; echocardiography is the screening test of choice and should be performed at diagnosis and again in convalescence.

stage5.final_clue: He is started on **high-dose IVIG (2 g/kg) plus high-dose aspirin**, with rapid defervescence within 24 hours. Over the next two weeks he develops desquamation of the fingertips and toes, and is maintained on low-dose aspirin with serial echocardiograms to monitor for coronary aneurysms.

debrief.classic_presentation: Kawasaki disease classically affects children under 5 years old (especially those of Japanese or East Asian descent). Diagnosis is clinical and requires fever lasting 5 or more days plus at least 4 of 5 principal features (mnemonic 'CRASH and BURN'): Conjunctivitis (bilateral, nonexudative, with limbal sparing), Rash (polymorphous), Adenopathy (cervical, often unilateral and >1.5 cm), Strawberry tongue / cracked lips / oral changes, Hand/foot erythema or edema with later desquamation, plus Burn (fever >5 days). The child is classically extremely irritable.
debrief.pathophysiology: Kawasaki disease is an acute self-limited medium-vessel vasculitis of unknown etiology, most likely triggered by an infectious agent in a genetically susceptible host. Immune activation produces systemic vasculitis with a predilection for the coronary arteries, where it can cause myocarditis, pancarditis, and, most importantly, coronary artery aneurysms and thrombosis. Coronary aneurysms develop in about 25% of untreated patients and can lead to myocardial infarction in childhood.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment with IVIG (2 g/kg as a single infusion) plus high-dose aspirin given within the first 10 days dramatically reduces the risk of coronary aneurysms. Aspirin is continued at a low anti-platelet dose until inflammatory markers normalize (and indefinitely if aneurysms develop). Echocardiography is performed at diagnosis, at 2 weeks, and at 6-8 weeks to screen for coronary involvement. This is one of the few indications for aspirin in children (otherwise avoided because of Reye syndrome).
high_yield_pearls: CRASH and BURN: Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue, Hand/foot changes, plus fever (Burn) >5 days.
Most common in children under 5, especially Asian descent; diagnosis is clinical.
Feared complication: coronary artery aneurysms with risk of MI in childhood.
Treatment = IVIG plus high-dose aspirin; one of the few accepted pediatric aspirin uses.
Labs show elevated ESR/CRP, thrombocytosis (especially in week 2), and often sterile pyuria.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Vasculitides — Kawasaki disease' section.
